name: Bulgogi (Korean Marinated Beef)
description: The classic. Sweet, savory, lightly smoky — one of the most approachable Korean dishes to make at home. Goes over rice, in lettuce wraps, or just straight out of the pan. Use lean beef cuts and trim visible fat if you want to keep it light.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Marinade
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together first

ingredients.name: Asian pear or apple
ingredients.note: grated; this is the tenderizer — don't skip it

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: convenient substitute

alternatives.name: jarred minced ginger
alternatives.note: or freeze a knob and grate from frozen

ingredientGroups.label: Protein & aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add to marinade, toss well, marinate 30 min–overnight

ingredients.name: beef
ingredients.note: ribeye, sirloin, or any thin-sliced cut

alternatives.name: bulgogi-cut beef from Korean grocery store
alternatives.note: comes pre-sliced, saves a lot of work

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add just before serving

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: optional accompaniments for serving

instructions.step: If beef isn't pre-sliced thin, partially freeze it (~30 min) then slice as thin as possible against the grain. Slice onion thinly.
Grate Asian pear/apple. Mince garlic and grate ginger. Mix soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, garlic, ginger, pear/apple, and pepper.
Add beef and onion. Mix well and marinate at least 30 min; overnight in the fridge is noticeably better.
Cook in a hot pan (or grill) in batches — don't overcrowd or it steams instead of sears. High heat, quick cook, ~2–3 min per batch.
Chop green onions. Finish with sesame seeds and green onion. Serve over rice.

protein.note: per serving; pair with a fried egg or tofu side for more

tips.text: The pear/apple is not optional — the enzymes genuinely tenderize the beef. No good substitutes.
Korean grocery stores sell pre-cut bulgogi beef which saves a lot of time.
Cook in a very hot, dry pan (or cast iron) for the best caramelization. A little char is good.

name: Dakgalbi (Korean Spicy Stir-Fried Chicken)
description: Classic Korean street food. Spicy, slightly sweet, smoky from the gochujang. Cabbage and sweet potato bulk it up without adding much fat. Use chicken breast to keep it lean — the sauce does all the heavy lifting.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Chicken & marinade
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, let sit 15+ min

ingredients.name: gochugaru
ingredients.note: Korean chili flakes — adjust to heat preference

alternatives.name: dry sherry
alternatives.note: works as a substitute for mirin or rice wine

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: convenient substitute

alternatives.name: jarred minced ginger
alternatives.note: or freeze a knob and grate from frozen

ingredientGroups.label: Vegetables
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added to the pan during cooking

ingredients.name: sweet potato or regular potato
ingredients.note: cut into thin rounds; optional but traditional

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add just before serving

instructions.step: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces. Mince garlic and grate ginger. Mix all marinade ingredients. Toss chicken and let sit at least 15 min (longer is better — up to overnight in the fridge).
Slice onion. Roughly chop cabbage. If using sweet potato, cut into thin rounds, then either microwave 2–3 min first to soften, or add to the pan early with a splash of water.
Heat pan on medium-high with a little oil. Add marinated chicken and onion.
Cook ~5 min, stirring occasionally. Add cabbage and sweet potato.
Stir-fry everything together until chicken is cooked through and cabbage is wilted (~8–10 min total).
Chop green onion. Top with green onion. Serve with rice or eat as-is.

protein.note: per serving with chicken breast + rice on the side

tips.text: Don't crowd the pan — you want some char on the chicken, not steaming.
Cheese topping (mozzarella) is popular in restaurants. Skip it to keep things lighter, or add it as a treat.
Leftover dakgalbi makes incredible fried rice the next day.

name: Doenjang Butter Pasta
description: A Korean riff on the miso butter pasta idea — but doenjang is earthier, funkier, and saltier than Japanese miso, so the result is deeper and bolder. Butter and starchy pasta water do all the sauce-building work here: no cream needed, no heavy roux. The emulsion comes together in under a minute and coats every strand. This is a pantry pasta — the only fresh item you really need is butter and pasta. Add mushrooms for more umami layers, or top with a soft-boiled egg for easy extra protein.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Doenjang sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together and set aside

ingredients.name: doenjang
ingredients.note: start with 1 tsp — taste and add more; it's saltier than miso

alternatives.name: salted butter
alternatives.note: skip any added salt elsewhere if using

ingredients.name: soy sauce
ingredients.note: optional — adds gloss and saltiness; doenjang alone may be enough

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked in butter before adding sauce

alternatives.name: onion, diced
alternatives.note: shallots are sweeter and melt down better

ingredientGroups.label: Pan additions
ingredientGroups.actionNote: optional but recommended — add after aromatics

ingredients.name: mushrooms, sliced
ingredients.note: shiitake, cremini, oyster — any work; shiitake is the best match

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: boil in salted water; reserve pasta water before draining

ingredients.name: pasta
ingredients.note: spaghetti, linguine, or any long pasta

ingredients.name: pasta water
ingredients.note: reserve before draining — this is the sauce

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: optional — pick one or combine

ingredients.name: cooked chicken breast or thigh, sliced or shredded

alternatives.name: shrimp, peeled
alternatives.note: cook in the same pan before the aromatics

alternatives.name: soft-boiled eggs, halved
alternatives.note: 6–7 min boil, ice bath, peel

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add at the end before serving

ingredients.name: parmesan or grana padano, grated
ingredients.note: optional — adds another umami layer, works surprisingly well

instructions.step: Boil pasta in salted water. Reserve at least ½ cup pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, if adding shrimp, cook in a lightly oiled pan over medium-high heat 2 min per side until pink. Set aside.
Slice shallots thin. Slice mushrooms if using. In the same pan over medium heat, melt 1 tbsp butter. Add shallots and cook ~3 min until soft and translucent. Add garlic, cook 1 more min.
Add mushrooms. Cook ~3–4 min until softened and starting to brown.
Lower heat to medium-low. Add the doenjang-butter mixture and stir to melt into the pan. Don't let it boil — lower heat if needed.
Add ¼ cup pasta water and stir. Add drained pasta directly to the pan. Toss to coat. Add more pasta water a splash at a time until the sauce clings to the pasta and looks glossy, not watery.
Slice or shred chicken if using. Add protein back in (shrimp, chicken) and toss. Taste — adjust with a tiny bit more doenjang or soy sauce if needed.
Slice green onion thin. Plate, top with soft-boiled egg if using, green onion, sesame seeds, gochugaru, and parmesan.

protein.note: pasta only with parmesan and egg; ~45–55g with chicken breast added; egg on top adds ~6g per egg

tips.keyword: Doenjang is not miso — use less
tips.text: It's saltier and funkier (fermented longer). Start with 1 tsp per 2 servings and taste. You can always add more; you can't take it out.

tips.keyword: Don't boil the sauce after adding doenjang
tips.text: High heat degrades the fermented flavor and can make the paste grainy. Medium-low once the paste goes in.
The starch emulsifies everything — butter, doenjang, sesame oil — into a silky coating. Don't drain it all; you'll almost certainly need more than you think.

tips.keyword: Shiitake mushrooms are the best match
tips.text: Their earthiness pairs with doenjang perfectly. Dried shiitake (rehydrated) work just as well and are shelf-stable.

tips.keyword: Parmesan and doenjang sound weird, taste great
tips.text: Both are fermented, umami-forward, funky. They get along well. Don't skip it if you have it.

name: Gochujang Chicken Pasta
description: A Korean-Italian mashup that actually works. Gochujang brings deep fermented heat, Greek yogurt keeps it creamy without heaviness. High protein, one pan (plus the pasta pot).

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

ingredients.name: Greek yogurt
ingredients.note: plain, full-fat or low-fat both work

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: season and pan-sear before making the sauce

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: go in the pan together

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: convenient substitute

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook in salted water; reserve pasta water before draining

ingredients.name: pasta
ingredients.note: rigatoni, cavatappi, or fusilli recommended; lower end for lighter days, higher end on training days

ingredientGroups.label: Cooking
ingredientGroups.actionNote: use as needed

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: optional — add at plating

instructions.step: Cook pasta in salted water. Reserve a cup of pasta water before draining.
Slice chicken thin against the grain. Season with a pinch of salt. Pan-sear in a lightly oiled pan over medium-high until cooked through (~4–5 min). Set aside.
Mince garlic and dice onion. In the same pan, sauté until softened (~2 min).
Add gochujang, soy sauce, and honey. Stir and cook 1 min.
Lower heat to medium-low. Add Greek yogurt and pasta water. Stir until a smooth sauce forms — don't let it boil or the yogurt will break.
Add pasta and chicken back in. Toss to coat. Add more pasta water if too thick.
Plate and top with green onion or sesame seeds if using.

protein.note: per serving with 350–400g chicken breast total + Greek yogurt

tips.text: Thin-sliced chicken cooks faster and stays tender. Slice against the grain.
Don't skip the pasta water — it's the glue that binds the sauce.
Gochujang varies in heat level by brand; taste and adjust.

notes: Made 4 servings (doubled). Sauce came out saucy and well-coated, not paste-like — consistency was good.

Went off-script with seasonings partway through — added more honey than specified, and more of most things in general. Still worked, so the recipe tolerates some looseness. Worth following the amounts more closely next time to get a proper baseline.

name: Gochujang Glazed Salmon
description: High-protein, weeknight-fast, and genuinely impressive-looking for how little effort it takes. The gochujang glaze — sweet, spicy, fermented — caramelizes against the salmon's natural fat to form a sticky, deeply savory crust. Serve over rice with a pile of green onion and you have a full meal in 20 minutes. The only real risk here is burning the glaze: keep the heat at medium, not high, and watch the last couple minutes closely.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Glaze
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

alternatives.name: fresh garlic
alternatives.note: minced

ingredients.name: mirin
ingredients.note: optional — adds gloss and a little sweetness

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: pat dry before cooking — moisture is the enemy of a good sear

ingredients.name: salmon
ingredients.note: skin-on or off — skin-on is easier to handle

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve the salmon on top

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add at plating

steps: Pat salmon dry with paper towel — moisture is the enemy of a good sear. Season lightly with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Mix glaze ingredients together.
Heat a pan over medium heat with a thin film of neutral oil.
Place salmon skin-side down (if skin-on) and cook ~4 min without moving.
Flip. Spoon or brush half the glaze over the cooked side.
Cook another 2 min. Add remaining glaze and let it bubble and caramelize ~1 min, tilting the pan to baste. Watch it — the sugar burns fast at this stage.
Rest 1–2 min before serving.
Pat salmon dry. Brush glaze generously on top and sides.
Air fry at 200°C (390°F) for 8–10 min depending on thickness. No flipping needed. Check at 8 min.
Pat salmon dry. Place on a foil-lined baking sheet. Brush glaze generously on top.
Broil on a rack ~15cm from the element for 8–10 min. Watch closely in the last 2 min — the glaze can char quickly.
Thinly slice green onions. Place salmon over rice. Spoon any pan glaze over the top. Finish with green onion, sesame seeds, and a squeeze of citrus if you have it.

protein.note: per serving with 2 fillets (~150–180g each); salmon is also rich in omega-3s and remains filling without being heavy

tips.text: The gochujang has sugar in it and the glaze scorches fast. If you see it going dark too quickly, pull the pan off the heat for a few seconds.
This is the difference between a sear and a steam. Takes 10 seconds and matters a lot.
The skin acts as a buffer — harder to overcook the flesh. Peel it off on the plate if you don't like eating it.
1–2 min off heat lets the carryover cooking finish gently and keeps the fish from falling apart on the plate.
Thaw overnight in the fridge or under cold running water. Salmon is one of the best freezer proteins.

name: Kimchi Fried Rice
description: The ultimate use-what-you-have Korean meal — deeply savory, a little spicy, and endlessly flexible depending on what protein you throw in. Day-old rice is non-negotiable here: fresh rice is too wet and steams instead of frying, giving you mush instead of the slightly crispy, individual grains you want. Gochujang adds fermented depth, kimchi juice does the heavy lifting on seasoning, and a fried egg on top turns it into a complete meal.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

ingredientGroups.label: Rice & Kimchi
ingredientGroups.actionNote: the base — have ready before cooking starts

ingredients.name: cooked rice
ingredients.note: day-old, refrigerated overnight — this is essential

ingredients.name: kimchi juice
ingredients.note: from the jar — the best free seasoning you have

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked first

ingredients.name: green onions
ingredients.note: white parts sliced thin; green parts reserved for garnish

alternatives.name: fresh garlic
alternatives.note: minced

ingredientGroups.label: Optional Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add early — cook through before rice goes in

ingredients.name: diced SPAM, bacon, leftover cooked meat, or canned tuna
ingredients.note: any protein works; tuna should be drained well

alternatives.name: skip protein
alternatives.note: still filling with an egg on top for a vegetarian version

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish & Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add at plating after heat is off

instructions.step: Heat a pan or wok over medium-high heat. Add a neutral oil (or a little from the kimchi jar).
If using protein, cook it first until browned. Remove and set aside.
Dice onion. Slice green onions, separating white and green parts. Roughly chop kimchi. Sauté onion and garlic until translucent, ~2 min. Add the white parts of green onion.
Add kimchi and cook ~2 min until it starts to caramelize slightly on the edges.
Push everything to the side. Add the cold rice to the pan and break it up with a spatula. Let it sit untouched ~1 min to get a little crust, then toss everything together.
Add the sauce and kimchi juice. Stir-fry 2–3 min until everything is well-coated and the rice looks glossy. Add protein back in and toss.
Turn off heat. Drizzle a little sesame oil and toss one more time.
Fry eggs separately to your liking (runny yolk is classic). Plate rice, top with fried egg, green onion tops, and sesame seeds.

protein.note: per serving with egg + SPAM or canned tuna; ~20–25g with egg only; double the protein or add two eggs to push higher

tips.keyword: Day-old rice is the whole game
tips.text: Fresh rice has too much moisture — it steams and clumps instead of frying. Always make extra rice the night before if you're planning this.
Don't skip it — it adds acidity, heat, and fermented funk that you can't replicate any other way.
If you add rice and there's too much steam, the rice won't crisp. Wok or large skillet at high heat is ideal.
Kimchi juice and soy sauce together are usually enough — add more only if needed.
Breaking a runny yolk over fried rice is the move. Hard yolk is fine but you miss the richness.

name: Kimchi Tuna Pasta
description: Fast, cheap, and punchy. Kimchi does most of the flavoring work — sour, fermented, spicy. Tuna keeps it high-protein with zero fuss. One of the best "nothing in the fridge" meals.

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: drain well before adding to the pan

alternatives.name: tuna in water
alternatives.note: use two cans to boost protein

ingredientGroups.label: Pan additions
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added in sequence during cooking

ingredients.name: kimchi
ingredients.note: roughly chopped; use well-fermented kimchi for better flavor

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: convenient substitute

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook in salted water; reserve pasta water before draining

ingredientGroups.label: Finish & garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add at the end after heat is off or just before serving

ingredients.name: sesame oil
ingredients.note: a few drops at the end

ingredients.name: egg
ingredients.note: soft poached or soft-boiled; optional but great

instructions.step: Cook pasta in salted water. Reserve pasta water before draining.
Mince garlic. Roughly chop kimchi. In a pan over medium heat, add a small amount of oil. Sauté garlic ~1 min.
Add kimchi and cook 2–3 min until slightly caramelized and fragrant.
Add tuna, gochujang if using, and soy sauce. Stir and cook another 1–2 min.
Add pasta and pasta water. Toss everything together.
Finish with a few drops of sesame oil. Top with soft egg and green onion.

protein.note: per serving with tuna + egg; use two cans of tuna to push higher

tips.text: Well-fermented (older, sour) kimchi is better here than fresh — it has more depth.
Don't over-rinse the tuna. You want some brine for flavor.
The egg on top is optional but transforms the dish — break it into the hot pasta.

name: Spicy Korean Pork (Daeji Bulgogi)
description: Daeji bulgogi is Korean street food at its best — thin pork strips in a bold, spicy-sweet marinade that caramelizes in a hot pan into something sticky and slightly charred at the edges. Where regular bulgogi is mild and family-friendly, this one brings the gochujang heat front and center. Pork belly is the classic, richer choice; shoulder is leaner and still great. Ground pork skips the marinate time entirely and works on a busy weeknight. Serve over rice or in lettuce wraps with sesame seeds and green onion.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Marinade
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together first

alternatives.name: jarred minced ginger
alternatives.note: or grate directly from a frozen knob

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: toss in marinade and marinate at least 30 min

ingredients.name: pork shoulder, thinly sliced
ingredients.note: lean, good caramelization — the everyday choice

alternatives.name: pork belly, thinly sliced
alternatives.note: fattier, richer, more traditional; trim if you want it lighter

alternatives.name: ground pork
alternatives.note: skip the marinate; cook pork first then add sauce — fastest version

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add to pan with the pork

ingredients.name: green onions, white parts sliced; green parts reserved for garnish

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish & Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve alongside or on top

alternatives.name: large lettuce leaves
alternatives.note: butter lettuce, romaine, or perilla for wraps

instructions.step: Mix all marinade ingredients together in a bowl.

Slice pork thinly. Slice onion thinly. Slice green onions, reserving green parts for garnish. Add pork and onion to the marinade. Mix well so everything is coated. Marinate at least 30 min at room temperature, or overnight in the fridge.
Heat a large pan or wok over high heat until very hot. Add a small amount of neutral oil.
Add pork in a single layer — cook in batches if needed. Don't overcrowd or the meat steams instead of searing. Let it sit 1–2 min before moving to get some caramelization.
Stir-fry ~4–5 min total until cooked through and edges are slightly charred. Add white parts of green onion in the last minute.
Plate over rice or serve with lettuce wraps. Top with green onion and sesame seeds.

steps: Cook ground pork in a hot pan, breaking it apart, until fully cooked and any moisture has evaporated (~5 min).
Mix marinade ingredients and pour directly over the cooked pork. Stir-fry another 2–3 min until the sauce cooks down and coats everything.

protein.note: per serving with pork shoulder; pork belly closer to 30–35g due to higher fat content; ground pork in between

tips.keyword: Thin slices are the whole point
tips.text: Thick slices won't caramelize the same way — the exterior dries out before the center cooks. Partially freeze the pork (~30 min) before slicing to make thin cuts much easier.
Shoulder is leaner and easier to keep light; belly is more indulgent. Neither is wrong — it just depends on what you're after that day.
One batch that sears beats two batches that steam. If the pan has too much moisture (steam rising), take some pork out and let the liquid cook off before adding it back.
A little dark caramelization on the edges is the goal — that's where all the sticky, sweet-spicy depth comes from. Don't be afraid of it.
If pork belly feels heavy on its own, wrapping it in cool lettuce with rice balances it out perfectly.
The gochujang and soy sauce penetrate deeper, and the pork softens slightly. If you have time, do it the night before.

name: Gyudon (Beef Rice Bowl)
description: Thinly sliced beef and onion simmered in a sweet-savory dashi broth, spooned over a bowl of rice — this is Japanese fast food done right at home. The broth does double duty, seasoning both the meat and soaking into the rice beneath it. Serve with a soft-boiled egg or raw egg yolk on top for the full yoshinoya experience.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Broth
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: use as-is; add directly to pan

ingredients.name: thinly sliced beef
ingredients.note: shabu-shabu or sukiyaki cut from Asian grocery

alternatives.name: frozen pre-sliced beef
alternatives.note: completely valid, often easier to find; thaw before use

alternatives.name: ribeye or sirloin block
alternatives.note: freeze ~30 min then slice as thin as possible with a sharp knife

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add right before serving

ingredients.name: soft-boiled egg or raw egg yolk

instructions.step: Mix all broth ingredients together in a small bowl and set aside.
Slice onion thin into half-moons. In a medium pan or skillet over medium heat, add the broth and onion. Bring to a simmer.
Cook onion ~3–4 min until softened and slightly translucent.
Add the beef slices, spreading them out so they cook evenly. Simmer ~3–4 min, skimming any foam that rises.
Taste the broth — it should be noticeably savory and slightly sweet. Adjust with a splash more soy sauce if needed.
Ladle the beef, onion, and plenty of broth over a bowl of rice.
Slice green onion thin. Top with green onion, pickled ginger, and egg if using. Eat immediately.

protein.note: per serving (200–250g beef); add a soft-boiled egg for an extra ~6g

tips.text: It's flavoring the rice underneath it, so if it tastes right on its own, it'll taste underseasoned on the bowl. Push it saltier and sweeter than you think.
Thick slices won't absorb the broth the same way and take longer to cook. Asian grocery freezer sections almost always have pre-sliced beef labelled "shabu-shabu" or "sukiyaki" — that's the one.
It's thin and goes from perfect to rubbery fast. Once it's no longer pink, it's done.
Melts into the hot broth and adds richness. Totally optional but worth trying once.

name: Karaage (Japanese Fried Chicken)
description: Juicy chicken thighs marinated in soy, sake, ginger, and garlic — coated in cornstarch and fried until shatteringly crispy. This is Japan's answer to fried chicken, and it's arguably better: the pieces are bite-sized, the crust is delicate and light (not bready), and the marinade punches all the way through. The air fryer method is the recommended approach here — it comes out surprisingly crispy with far less mess and oil.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Marinade
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together and add chicken to marinate

alternatives.name: mirin
alternatives.note: slightly sweeter, works well

alternatives.name: dry sherry or rice wine vinegar
alternatives.note: in a pinch

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: fine here since it's in a marinade

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cut into bite-sized pieces before marinating

alternatives.name: skinless chicken thighs
alternatives.note: still great, just slightly less crispy exterior

ingredientGroups.label: Coating
ingredientGroups.actionNote: apply just before cooking — do not coat in advance

alternatives.name: potato starch (katakuriko)
alternatives.note: traditional Japanese choice, slightly lighter and crispier

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve alongside

steps: Cut chicken into 4–5cm bite-sized pieces. Grate ginger and mince garlic. Mix all marinade ingredients in a bowl. Add chicken, toss to coat, and marinate at least 20 min. 1–2 hours is better; overnight in the fridge is best.
Preheat air fryer to 200°C (400°F).
Remove chicken from marinade, shaking off excess liquid. Toss pieces in cornstarch until fully coated — a light, even layer.
Arrange in a single layer in the air fryer basket. Don't crowd; do two batches if needed.
Cook 18–20 min, flipping halfway through, until golden and crispy.
Serve immediately with Kewpie mayo, lemon, and cabbage.
Heat neutral oil (vegetable, canola, sunflower) in a pot or deep pan to 170°C (340°F). Use enough oil to submerge the pieces.
Coat chicken in cornstarch as above.
Fry in batches ~4–5 min per batch until light golden. Don't crowd the pot.
Remove and rest on a wire rack 2–3 min. For extra crispiness, raise oil to 190°C (375°F) and fry again ~1–2 min until deep golden and shatteringly crispy.
Serve immediately.

protein.note: per serving (200–250g chicken thigh per serving)

tips.text: Flour makes fried chicken bready and heavy. Cornstarch creates a thin, crispy, almost glass-like shell. Don't substitute flour.
Shake off marinade excess or pat lightly before coating — too much liquid prevents the coating from sticking and crisping.
20 min is the minimum. The soy-sake marinade is doing real flavor work, not just surface seasoning.
A light spray of neutral oil on top of the coated pieces before cooking helps achieve a more even golden color.
The first fry cooks the chicken through at lower temp; the second fry at high heat crisps the crust. Worth doing if you're already frying in oil.

name: Katsu (Breaded Chicken or Pork Cutlet)
description: A thin cutlet of chicken or pork, coated in the classic flour → egg → panko sequence and pan-fried to a golden, crackly crust. Served with tonkatsu sauce, a mound of shredded cabbage, and rice — it's filling, high-protein, and way more satisfying than it has any right to be for how simple it is. Panko (not regular breadcrumbs) is what makes the crust light and airy instead of dense and bready.

alternatives.name: chicken thighs
alternatives.note: harder to pound evenly but more forgiving and juicier

alternatives.name: pork loin chops (150–180g each), pounded to ~1.5cm thickness
