alternatives.note: the standard version; sold at any Asian grocery — better call if you have it

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve alongside

ingredients.name: shredded raw cabbage
ingredients.note: a generous pile — this is not optional garnish, it's part of the dish

instructions.step: Pound the meat to ~1.5cm thickness using a meat mallet, heavy pan, or rolling pin. Even thickness means even cooking.
Season lightly with salt and pepper on both sides.
Set up the breading station — flour on one plate, beaten egg in a bowl, panko on another plate.
Coat each piece — press into flour (shake off excess) → dip in egg (let excess drip off) → press firmly into panko on both sides. The panko should fully cover with no bare spots.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat. Test readiness by dropping a panko crumb in — it should sizzle immediately.
Add the cutlet. Pan-fry ~3–4 min per side until deep golden brown. Don't press down or move it — let it sit.
Transfer to a wire rack or paper towel to drain briefly.
Slice into strips, plate with cabbage and rice, and spoon tonkatsu sauce over the top.

steps: After breading, spray the cutlet generously on both sides with neutral cooking oil spray.
Cook at 200°C (400°F) for 15–18 min, flipping once halfway. The crust won't be as deeply golden as pan-fried but is still very good.

protein.note: per serving with chicken breast; pork loin is similar at ~40–50g

tips.text: This is the most important step. Uneven thickness means one part overcooks while another stays raw. Use a zip-lock bag around the meat to avoid mess.
Regular fine breadcrumbs make a dense, greasy crust. Panko is coarser, creates air pockets, and stays crispy longer after cooking.
Into the meat — don't just roll it. Pieces that fall off during frying are pieces that weren't pressed in.
Too low and the crust absorbs oil and goes soggy. Too high and it burns before the meat cooks through. Medium heat with a properly pre-heated pan is the target.
The raw, crunchy cabbage cuts the richness of the katsu. Pile it high.

name: Miso Salmon Pasta
description: Light, clean, and surprisingly rich-tasting without being heavy. Miso and salmon are a natural pairing — the umami of the miso amplifies the fish without masking it. Great omega-3s and high protein, all in a 25-minute weeknight dish.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Miso sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

ingredients.name: white miso paste
ingredients.note: shiro miso — milder; use red miso for stronger flavor

ingredients.name: salmon fillet, skin on or off

alternatives.name: canned salmon
alternatives.note: drain well — works in a pinch, noticeably less good but shelf-stable

ingredients.name: black pepper
ingredients.note: for seasoning the salmon — no salt, miso is salty

ingredientGroups.label: Pan additions
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added during cooking

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: convenient substitute

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook pasta and reserve water before draining

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: optional — add at the end to taste

instructions.step: Cook pasta in salted water. Reserve pasta water before draining.
Season salmon with black pepper (no salt — miso is salty). Pan-sear skin-side down over medium-high heat ~3–4 min, flip and cook another 2 min. Set aside and flake into chunks.
Mince garlic. Slice mushrooms if using. In the same pan, sauté garlic ~1 min. Add mushrooms and cook until softened.
Lower heat. Dissolve miso paste in milk by stirring (or mix them in a small bowl first). Add to the pan with pasta water.
Add spinach if using — it wilts in ~30 seconds.
Toss pasta in the sauce. Fold in flaked salmon gently.
Taste and adjust — a few drops of soy sauce or a squeeze of lemon can brighten it up.

protein.note: per serving

tips.keyword: Don't boil the sauce after adding miso
tips.text: Boiling kills the probiotic content and can make the sauce grainy. Lower the heat before adding the miso mixture.
Canned salmon works in a pinch (drain well), though fresh is noticeably better.
White miso is milder and sweeter; red miso is saltier and more intense. Either works here.

name: Oyakodon (Chicken & Egg Rice Bowl)
description: "Parent and child bowl" — chicken and egg, cooked together in a savory dashi broth, served over rice. One of the most comforting Japanese home-cooking dishes and genuinely fast to make. Light, deeply savory, and high protein.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Broth
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

ingredients.name: dashi stock
ingredients.note: instant dashi powder + water is fine, widely available at Asian grocery stores

alternatives.name: 1 tsp sugar + a splash of sake or dry white wine
alternatives.note: or just add an extra tsp of sugar and skip the sake

ingredients.name: chicken breast or thigh, cut into bite-sized pieces
ingredients.note: thigh is juicier; breast is leaner

ingredientGroups.label: Eggs
ingredientGroups.actionNote: beat lightly just before using — some streaks of white and yolk is correct

ingredients.name: eggs
ingredients.note: per serving — yes, 3; this dish is egg-forward

instructions.step: Mix broth ingredients in a small bowl. Set aside.
Slice onion thin. In a medium pan (or small sauté pan), add the broth and onion. Bring to a simmer over medium heat.
Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces. Add chicken. Cook ~4–5 min until mostly cooked through.
Beat eggs lightly — you don't want them fully homogenized; some streaks of white and yolk is correct.
Pour eggs over the chicken and onion in a circular motion. Cover with a lid.
Cook ~1–2 min until eggs are just barely set — you want them soft and custardy, not fully cooked. This is the make-or-break step.
Slice green onion thin. Slide over a bowl of rice. Garnish with green onion. Eat immediately.

protein.note: per serving (3 eggs + chicken breast)

tips.text: The eggs should be soft and slightly runny in the center — think loose scramble, not frittata. Pull the pan off heat while they still look underdone; they finish from residual heat.
Instant dashi (hondashi) is easy to find at any Asian grocery. It's a pantry essential for Japanese cooking.
If you don't have mirin, substitute with 1 tsp sugar + a splash of sake or white wine. In a pinch, skip the sake.

name: Soba Noodles (Zaru Soba — Cold Dipping Noodles)
description: Cold soba is one of the most elegant simple dishes in Japanese cooking — chilled buckwheat noodles served on a plate, dipped into a cold savory tsuyu sauce with wasabi and green onion stirred in. It's light, clean, high in protein if you add toppings, and genuinely refreshing. The tsuyu sauce is three pantry ingredients plus water; or buy bottled mentsuyu from any Asian grocery and dilute it in 30 seconds. Either way works.

ingredientGroups.label: Noodles
ingredientGroups.actionNote: boil, then rinse cold and drain

ingredients.name: dried soba noodles
ingredients.note: buckwheat noodles — sold at any Asian grocery or well-stocked supermarket

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Tsuyu dipping sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: make ahead and chill; needs to be cold when served

ingredients.name: dashi stock
ingredients.note: ½ tsp hondashi powder dissolved in 200ml water

alternatives.name: bottled mentsuyu (tsuyu concentrate)
alternatives.note: dilute per bottle instructions (typically 1 part tsuyu to 3 parts water for dipping) — a completely valid shortcut, often better than homemade

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish / Dipping additions
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add to tsuyu or serve alongside

ingredients.name: wasabi (tube wasabi is fine)
ingredients.note: stir a small amount into the tsuyu

ingredientGroups.label: Protein boost
ingredientGroups.actionNote: optional — pick one; serve on top of the noodles

ingredients.name: cold poached chicken breast, sliced thin

instructions.section: Make the Tsuyu (skip if using bottled)
steps: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a small saucepan. Bring to a brief simmer over medium heat, stirring until sugar dissolves (~2 min). Remove from heat and let cool completely, then refrigerate until cold. Can be made 1–2 days ahead.
Bring a large pot of unsalted water to a boil. Add soba noodles and cook per package instructions — typically 4–5 min. Soba goes mushy fast, so start checking 1 min early.

Immediately drain and rinse aggressively under cold running water, rubbing the noodles together with your hands. Rinse until the noodles are fully chilled and the water runs clear. This removes excess starch and stops cooking.
Drain well and arrange on a plate or in a bowl.
Pour chilled tsuyu into a small bowl or cup for dipping.
Slice green onions thin. Add green onion and a small dab of wasabi to the tsuyu. Stir.
Dip bundles of soba into the tsuyu and eat. Add nori and daikon on top of the noodles if using.
Add protein on top of the noodles if boosting.
Skip chilling the tsuyu. Instead, thin it with an extra 150–200ml of hot dashi to make a light broth.
Serve the hot noodles in the broth (don't rinse them cold). Top with green onion, a soft-boiled egg, or sliced chicken.

protein.note: soba noodles alone — buckwheat is relatively protein-rich; add cold chicken (+30–35g) or a soft-boiled egg (+6g) to hit a meaningful target

tips.text: This is the single most common mistake. Soba turns mushy within a minute of being overdone. Set a timer and taste early.
The cold-water rinse is not optional — it stops cooking, removes surface starch (which makes noodles clump), and chills the noodles for serving. Rub them between your palms under the water.
Bottled tsuyu concentrate (mentsuyu) is sold at any Asian grocery and tastes great — often better than homemade unless you're using real kombu dashi. Just dilute per the bottle.
The soba should be partially dipped into the tsuyu, not submerged. You can adjust wasabi intensity in the dipping bowl to taste.
Make a double batch and refrigerate — it keeps 4–5 days and works as a sauce for other dishes (drizzle over tofu, use as a quick noodle sauce, etc.).

name: Teriyaki Chicken Udon
description: Simple weeknight stir-fry. The teriyaki sauce is just three ingredients and comes together in minutes. Udon noodles are satisfying without being heavy, and the vegetables add crunch and volume.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Teriyaki sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, set aside

alternatives.name: firm tofu, cubed
alternatives.note: replace or supplement the chicken — same sauce, works well

ingredientGroups.label: Vegetables
ingredientGroups.actionNote: stir-fried in this order

ingredientGroups.label: Noodles
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook according to package instructions, drain and set aside

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add right before serving

instructions.step: Cook udon according to package instructions. Drain and set aside.
Slice chicken thin. In a hot pan with minimal oil, cook chicken until cooked through (~4–5 min). Set aside.
Slice onion and julienne or slice carrot thin. Add onion and carrot to the pan. Stir-fry ~2–3 min.
Roughly chop cabbage. Add and cook another 1–2 min.
Add chicken back in, then the udon. Pour teriyaki sauce over everything.
Toss and stir-fry over high heat ~1–2 min until everything is coated and slightly caramelized.
Chop green onions. Top with green onion. Serve immediately.

protein.note: per serving; add a soft-boiled or fried egg on top for an easy boost

tips.text: High heat is key — you want some caramelization on the sauce, not just steaming.
Fresh udon from the refrigerator section is much better than dried. Most Asian grocery stores carry it.
Tofu can replace or supplement the chicken for additional protein variety.

name: Larb (Thai/Lao Minced Meat Salad)
description: One of the most protein-dense, low-fat dishes you can make. It's essentially seasoned ground meat tossed with herbs, lime juice, and toasted rice powder. No heavy sauces, no cream, no oil to speak of — just pure flavor from the aromatics and fish sauce. Incredibly light, satisfying, and fast.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Dressing
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together and set aside

alternatives.name: bottled lime juice
alternatives.note: works here since it's being dressed into the meat, not used as a raw finishing squeeze

ingredients.name: toasted rice powder
ingredients.note: see tips for how to make this — do not skip

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked with a splash of water, no oil; drain liquid after

ingredients.name: ground chicken or pork
ingredients.note: both are traditional; chicken is leaner

ingredientGroups.label: Herbs & aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: toss in right before serving

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve alongside

alternatives.name: lettuce leaves
alternatives.note: for a lighter, lower-carb option

instructions.step: Cook ground meat in a pan over medium heat with a splash of water (no oil needed). Break it up and cook until done. Drain off any liquid.
Transfer to a mixing bowl. Let cool slightly — blazing hot will wilt the herbs immediately.
Add fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder. Toss well.
Slice shallots thin and slice green onions. Add shallots, mint, cilantro, and green onion. Toss again.
Taste — it should be sour, salty, herbaceous, with a background heat. Adjust fish sauce (salty), lime (sour), or chili as needed.
Serve immediately with rice or lettuce.

protein.note: per serving; almost entirely from lean ground meat — very favorable macro profile

tips.text: Put a small handful of uncooked jasmine rice in a dry pan. Toast over medium heat, stirring, until golden brown and nutty-smelling (~5–7 min). Grind in a spice grinder or with a mortar and pestle. It adds a subtle nutty crunch and helps absorb the dressing. Make a batch and store it.
The herbs wilt quickly after dressing. Dress and serve immediately.
Based on how salty your fish sauce is. Start conservatively and build up.
Some versions add thin-sliced raw shallot for extra crunch and bite — worth trying.

name: Pad Kra Pao (Thai Basil Ground Meat)
description: Thailand's most popular everyday dish. Aggressively flavored — garlic, chili, fish sauce, oyster sauce — and ready in 20 minutes. Ground meat makes this ideal for the fat-drain technique; drain and rinse before adding to the pan, then let the Thai seasonings do the rest. Always served with a fried egg on top. Non-negotiable.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together and set aside before cooking

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook first, drain fat, return to pan

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added to the pan with the drained meat, stir-fried on high heat

ingredients.name: Thai bird's eye chilies, minced
ingredients.note: adjust to heat preference — this dish should be spicy

alternatives.name: red chili flakes or cayenne powder
alternatives.note: less fresh heat but works

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added off heat — residual heat wilts it in ~30 seconds

ingredients.name: Thai basil leaves (holy basil / krapao is authentic)
ingredients.note: regular Italian basil works in a pinch but tastes different

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: fry egg separately; serve over rice with egg on top

ingredients.name: fried egg (over-easy or sunny side up, runny yolk)

instructions.step: Cook ground meat in a pan over medium-high heat. Break it up as it cooks.
Drain fat (and rinse if you prefer), then return to pan.
Mince garlic and mince chilies. Add garlic and chili directly to the meat. Stir-fry 1–2 min over high heat.
Add fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sugar. Toss to coat everything.
Turn off heat. Add Thai basil and toss — the residual heat wilts it in ~30 seconds.
Fry an egg separately (over-easy or sunny side up, runny yolk). Serve over rice with the egg on top and the yolk running into the meat.

protein.note: per serving with ground chicken or beef plus egg

tips.text: Is the authentic choice and tastes noticeably different from Italian basil. Worth seeking out at Asian grocery stores if you can.

tips.keyword: The fried egg is not garnish
tips.text: It's structural. The runny yolk mixes into the meat and rice and ties everything together.
This dish should be cooked on high heat the whole time. You want some char, not steaming.
If the sauce gets too thick or sticky, a small splash of water loosens it immediately.

name: Tom Yum Chicken
description: Hot and sour Thai soup. Clear broth, intensely aromatic — lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, lime. Not heavy at all; it's basically a broth with protein and mushrooms. The aromatics are the hard part — finding them. Once you have them it's just assembling and simmering.

ingredientGroups.label: Pot — Broth base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: bring to simmer before adding aromatics

alternatives.name: water + 1 bouillon cube
alternatives.note: dissolve cube in hot water first

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics bundle
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add to broth together; not eaten — remove or avoid when serving

ingredients.name: lemongrass, cut into 2-inch pieces and lightly bruised
ingredients.note: freeze immediately after buying — use straight from frozen

ingredients.name: galangal
ingredients.note: freeze if you buy it; use straight from frozen

alternatives.name: fresh ginger
alternatives.note: different flavor but works

ingredients.name: kaffir lime leaves, torn
ingredients.note: freeze immediately — use straight from frozen

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add after aromatics have infused; thin slices cook fast

alternatives.name: shrimp
alternatives.note: faster-cooking; add in last 2–3 min only

ingredientGroups.label: Vegetables
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added before the protein

ingredientGroups.label: Seasoning
ingredientGroups.actionNote: stir in near the end; taste and adjust

alternatives.name: bottled lime juice
alternatives.note: fine for a cooked soup

ingredients.name: Thai roasted chili paste (nam prik pao)
ingredients.note: optional but recommended — adds depth and color

ingredientGroups.label: Garnish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add at serving

instructions.step: Cut lemongrass into 2-inch pieces and bruise lightly. Tear kaffir lime leaves. Halve chilies. Bring broth to a simmer in a medium pot. Add lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and chilies. Simmer 10–15 min to infuse.
Slice mushrooms if needed. Add mushrooms and cook ~3 min.
Slice chicken thin. Add chicken and cook until just done, ~3–4 min. Don't overcook — thin slices go fast.
Stir in fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, and roasted chili paste if using.
Taste — it should be assertively sour and salty. Adjust fish sauce and lime as needed.
Ladle into bowls. Top with cilantro and green onion. Eat with rice or just the soup.

protein.note: per serving

tips.keyword: The aromatics are for flavor only
tips.text: Lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves are tough and fibrous — don't eat them. They're in the soup to infuse flavor into the broth.
All three (lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves) are available at any Korean or Asian grocery and freeze well. Buy extra and freeze immediately — use straight from frozen.

tips.keyword: Nam prik pao makes a difference
tips.text: Roasted chili paste in a jar adds a smoky depth that elevates the soup significantly. It's inexpensive and lasts a long time in the fridge.
This soup is low in fat and calories — perfect for a high-protein, low-calorie meal or post-workout.

name: Aglio e Olio with Shrimp
description: The simplest serious pasta dish. No cream, no heavy sauce — just garlic, olive oil, chili flakes, pasta water, and parsley. Adding shrimp turns it into a high-protein meal that still feels light and clean. The technique is everything here: the emulsified sauce (garlic oil + starchy pasta water) coats the noodles without being greasy.

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: season before searing

alternatives.name: chicken breast
alternatives.note: sliced thin, seared separately — same dish, more protein

ingredientGroups.label: Sauce base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: built in the pan over low heat

ingredients.name: garlic, thinly sliced
ingredients.note: thin slices, not minced — this matters; use fresh garlic only, jarred won't slice and burns differently

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook in well-salted water; reserve pasta water before draining — this is the sauce base

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added off heat at the very end

ingredients.name: lemon zest or a squeeze of lemon

instructions.step: Cook pasta in well-salted water. Reserve at least a cup of pasta water before draining.
Season shrimp with salt and pepper. Quickly sear in a hot pan with minimal oil ~1 min per side. Set aside (they'll finish later). Don't overcook.

Slice garlic thin. Lower heat to medium-low. In the same pan, add olive oil and garlic slices. Cook slowly until the garlic is golden and fragrant (~3–4 min). Watch it carefully — golden is right, brown is bitter.
Add chili flakes and cook 30 seconds.
Add pasta water (start with 4 tbsp). Swirl the pan — the oil and water will emulsify into a light sauce.
Add pasta and toss vigorously for 1–2 min. Add more pasta water as needed to keep it saucy.
Add shrimp back in. Toss to coat and warm through.
Roughly chop parsley. Off heat, add parsley (and lemon if using). Taste and adjust salt.

protein.note: per serving

tips.text: The pasta water is not optional — it's what makes the sauce bind. Reserve more than you think you'll need.
Thin garlic slices (not minced) toast more evenly and have a mellower flavor. Minced garlic burns too easily.
Chicken breast (sliced thin, seared separately) is an easy swap or addition if you want even more protein.
Don't dress this with parmesan — it's not traditional here and doesn't pair well with olive oil-based sauces.

name: Bolognese
description: Bolognese is a slow-cooked meat sauce — humble ingredients, but the long, low simmer turns them into something deeply rich and silky. The technique is less about precision and more about patience: brown the meat well, build the base, add liquid in stages, then walk away and let it go. Whole milk sounds strange but it tenderizes the meat and rounds out any sharpness from the tomatoes. This makes a large batch by design — it freezes perfectly and is even better the next day.

ingredientGroups.label: Soffritto
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked low and slow in olive oil until completely soft — the flavor base

ingredients.name: garlic, minced
ingredients.note: optional but recommended — add after the soffritto softens

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: add with the vegetables, not after

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: browned in the pot after the soffritto, high heat, in batches if needed

ingredients.name: ground beef (80/20 fat ratio ideal)
ingredients.note: leaner beef dries out in the long simmer

alternatives.name: ground beef + ground pork
alternatives.note: the traditional mix; slightly richer and more complex

ingredientGroups.label: Sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added to the pot after meat is browned; milk goes in last 20–30 min

alternatives.name: beef stock
alternatives.note: adds depth without the acidity; wine is better if you have it

alternatives.name: whole peeled canned tomatoes, crushed by hand

ingredients.name: whole milk
ingredients.note: added in the last 20–30 minutes of simmering only

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked separately in salted water; tossed with sauce at serving

ingredients.name: tagliatelle or pappardelle
ingredients.note: wide flat pasta is traditional and grips the sauce beautifully

alternatives.name: rigatoni
alternatives.note: holds sauce well and easier to find

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: grate fresh at the table

instructions.step: Finely dice onion, carrots, and celery. Mince garlic. Heat olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Add onion, carrot, and celery. Cook slowly, stirring occasionally, until completely softened and slightly sweet — about 10–12 minutes. Don't rush this.
Add garlic (if using fresh) and cook another 1–2 minutes.

Increase heat to medium-high. Add ground meat. Break it up and cook without stirring too much — let it brown properly, not steam. Season with salt and pepper. This takes about 8–10 minutes. Drain excess fat if there's a lot.
Add tomato paste and stir into the meat. Cook for 2 minutes — it should darken slightly and coat everything.
Pour in the red wine. Scrape any browned bits off the bottom of the pot. Let it bubble and reduce by half, ~3–4 minutes.
Add crushed tomatoes. Stir to combine. Bring to a gentle simmer.

Reduce heat to low. Cover partially (lid slightly ajar) and simmer for 1–1.5 hours, stirring every 20–30 minutes. The sauce should be slowly bubbling, not rolling. Add a splash of water or stock if it gets too thick before the time is up.
In the last 20–30 minutes, pour in the whole milk. Stir in and continue to simmer uncovered until absorbed. The sauce will look slightly lighter — this is correct.
Taste and adjust salt. The sauce should be rich, slightly chunky, and not watery.

Cook pasta in heavily salted water. Reserve some pasta water. Drain, add pasta to the sauce, and toss briefly with a splash of pasta water to loosen and coat. Don't drown the pasta — use the sauce as a coating, not a pool.
Serve with grated parmesan.

protein.note: per serving; ground beef is naturally high protein — this dish hits targets without modification

tips.text: Vegetables cooked 10+ minutes on medium-low become sweet and almost melty — they disappear into the sauce and become its backbone. Rushed, they taste raw and harsh.

tips.keyword: Brown the meat, don't steam it
tips.text: High heat and not stirring too much = browning. Overcrowding the pan or stirring constantly = grey steamed meat. Brown = flavor.
It sounds wrong but it chemically tenderizes the meat proteins and buffers the acidity of the tomatoes. Add it. You won't taste milk — you'll taste a smoother, richer sauce.
Bolognese is a meat sauce that coats pasta, not a pool of liquid. Toss the pasta in the sauce — every strand should be coated but there shouldn't be a puddle.
Make a full batch, portion into containers, and freeze. It reheats beautifully with a splash of water or stock.

name: Cacio e Pepe
description: Three ingredients, one technique, infinite ways to get it wrong. Cacio e pepe is just pasta, pecorino romano, parmesan, and black pepper — but the technique is everything. The cheese has to be whisked into cold pasta water into a smooth paste before it ever touches heat, otherwise it seizes into a clumpy mess. Toast the pepper dry in the pan before anything else — this is what gives it that bold, punchy warmth that makes the dish. Get the technique right once and you'll make this constantly.

ingredientGroups.label: Cheese paste
ingredientGroups.actionNote: made in a bowl with cold pasta water — do this off heat, never in the pan

ingredients.name: pecorino romano, very finely grated
ingredients.note: microplane or fine box grater — this is critical

ingredients.name: cold pasta water
ingredients.note: added gradually to form a smooth, pourable paste

ingredientGroups.label: Pepper base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: toasted dry in the pan — the very first thing you do

ingredients.name: coarsely cracked black pepper
ingredients.note: freshly cracked, not pre-ground — the flavor is completely different

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: boil in lightly salted water (cheese is very salty — use less salt than usual)

ingredients.name: spaghetti or rigatoni
ingredients.note: tonnarelli (thick square spaghetti) is traditional; spaghetti works well; rigatoni holds up nicely

instructions.step: Bring a large pot of lightly salted water to a boil. Use slightly less salt than usual — the cheese is very salty.
Cook pasta until just al dente. Reserve at least 1 full cup of pasta water — use some immediately (it needs to be hot when you mix it into the cheese).
While pasta cooks, crack or coarsely grind the black pepper. Toast it dry in a large pan over medium heat for ~1 minute until fragrant. Remove from heat.

In a bowl, combine finely grated pecorino and parmesan. Add 3–4 tbsp of hot pasta water gradually, whisking constantly, until a smooth, creamy paste forms. It should be pourable, not thick. If it seizes, add more hot water.
Drain pasta (keep your pasta water). Add pasta to the pan with the toasted pepper over low heat. Add a splash of pasta water and toss to coat.
Take the pan fully off heat. Wait 15–20 seconds to let it cool slightly — this is important.
Pour the cheese paste over the pasta. Toss constantly and vigorously, adding pasta water a splash at a time to keep it moving. The sauce should turn glossy and creamy and coat every strand.
Plate immediately. Crack a little more black pepper on top if desired.

protein.note: from cheese only; low on its own — add a fried egg (+7g) or serve alongside grilled chicken (+30–40g)

tips.keyword: The cheese paste step is non-negotiable
tips.text: Throwing grated cheese directly into the hot pan is the number one mistake — it clumps instantly. Always make the paste with cold-to-warm water off heat first.
Dry-toasting whole cracked pepper for 60 seconds unlocks a completely different flavor — floral, pungent, warm. Don't skip it.
You're too hot. Pull the pan off heat, add more pasta water, and toss harder. The sauce recovers if you catch it early.

tips.keyword: If the sauce is too thin
tips.text: Add more cheese paste. If too thick, add a small splash of pasta water and toss.
Coarser cheese won't dissolve into the paste smoothly. A microplane is ideal.

name: Carbonara
description: The most technically demanding weeknight pasta — but once you get it, it's untouchable. Guanciale (or pancetta or bacon) renders down in its own fat, then pasta and a whisked egg-and-pecorino mixture get tossed together off the heat, using the residual warmth and starchy pasta water to emulsify into a glossy, silky sauce. No cream. Never cream. The whole point is that eggs and cheese become the sauce — and if you keep the heat off, it works perfectly every time.

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: rendered in the pan first — the fat becomes the cooking fat for everything else

ingredients.name: guanciale, cut into lardons or cubes

alternatives.name: pancetta
alternatives.note: very similar, slightly less funky; nearly identical result

alternatives.name: bacon
alternatives.note: more widely available; smokier flavor, works great

ingredientGroups.label: Egg mixture
ingredientGroups.actionNote: whisked together in a bowl before you start cooking — must be ready to go

ingredients.name: pecorino romano, very finely grated
ingredients.note: the finer the better — it needs to melt into the sauce

alternatives.name: parmesan
alternatives.note: milder and slightly less sharp; works well

alternatives.name: half pecorino, half parmesan
alternatives.note: the classic restaurant approach

ingredients.name: freshly cracked black pepper
ingredients.note: generous amount — this is a flavor, not a seasoning

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook until 1–2 min before al dente; it finishes in the pan

ingredients.name: rigatoni or spaghetti
ingredients.note: rigatoni holds the sauce inside the tubes and is highly recommended; spaghetti is more traditional

instructions.step: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a boil. Cook pasta until 1–2 minutes before al dente (it will finish in the pan).
Very finely grate pecorino and parmesan. While pasta cooks, whisk together eggs, egg yolks, grated cheese, and black pepper in a bowl. Set aside at room temperature — cold eggs scramble faster.

Cut guanciale (or substitute) into lardons or cubes. Cook in a large pan or skillet over medium heat until the fat has rendered and the meat is golden and slightly crispy, ~5–7 min. Turn off the heat and leave the meat and fat in the pan.
Reserve at least 1 full cup of pasta water before draining. Drain pasta.
Add pasta directly to the pan with the guanciale. Toss to coat in the fat. The pan should still be warm but off heat — wait 30 seconds if it's steaming too aggressively.

Pour the egg-and-cheese mixture over the pasta. Immediately toss constantly and vigorously, adding pasta water splash by splash (~4–6 tbsp total) to loosen the sauce. The starchy water helps the emulsion form.
Keep tossing until the sauce is glossy and clings to every piece of pasta — not wet, not dry. Add more pasta water if it tightens up too much.
Taste and adjust salt (the guanciale and cheese are already salty — you may not need any).
Plate immediately. Crack more black pepper over the top.

protein.note: per serving; guanciale or bacon ~15–20g, eggs ~12g, pecorino ~10g

tips.keyword: never add the egg mixture over direct heat
tips.text: This is the single rule. The eggs need warmth, not heat — off heat + pasta water is enough. If the eggs scramble, the dish is over.
Room temperature eggs emulsify more easily and are less likely to seize when they hit the warm pasta. Take them out when you start cooking.

tips.keyword: reserve more pasta water than you think
tips.text: You'll need at least 4–6 tbsp for the sauce, but keep a full cup on hand in case the sauce tightens.
Coarsely grated cheese won't melt into the sauce and will clump. A microplane or fine box grater works best.

tips.keyword: the sauce should be glossy, not runny
tips.text: If it's too loose, toss for another 30 seconds — the starch will thicken it. If it's too tight, add a splash more pasta water.

name: Chicken Piccata
description: Bright, lemony, slightly briny from capers. The sauce is built in the pan after searing the chicken — deglazing with wine or broth, adding lemon juice and capers. It's one of those dishes that tastes way more involved than it is. Lean, flavorful, light. Works over pasta or with a simple salad.

ingredientGroups.label: Protein prep
ingredientGroups.actionNote: season and dredge before searing

ingredients.name: chicken breasts, halved horizontally and pounded thin

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added to pan after removing chicken

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: convenient substitute, works fine here

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Pan sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: pre-measure, added to pan in sequence

alternatives.name: chicken broth
alternatives.note: less complex but still good — use all broth if skipping wine

alternatives.name: bouillon cube dissolved in hot water
alternatives.note: 1 cube dissolved in 100ml hot water

alternatives.name: bottled lemon juice
alternatives.note: fine since it's cooked into the sauce

ingredientGroups.label: Cooking
ingredientGroups.actionNote: heat before adding chicken

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added off heat to finish the sauce

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.note: a small knob — optional, adds richness, keep it small

instructions.step: Halve chicken breasts horizontally and pound to even thickness. Season with salt and pepper. Dredge lightly in flour, shaking off excess.
Heat olive oil in a pan over medium-high. Sear chicken cutlets ~3 min per side until golden and cooked through. Remove and set aside.
Mince garlic. Lower heat to medium. Add garlic and cook 30 seconds.
Deglaze with white wine, scraping up any browned bits. Let reduce by half (~2 min).
Add chicken broth, lemon juice, and capers. Simmer 3–4 min.
Swirl in butter if using (off heat). Taste and adjust — more lemon if it needs brightness, more salt if flat.
Chop parsley. Return chicken to the pan and spoon sauce over it. Finish with parsley.

protein.note: per serving with two chicken breast cutlets

tips.text: Pounding the chicken thin (between plastic wrap or in a zip-lock bag) ensures it cooks evenly and fast. Skip this and you'll end up with dry edges and an undercooked center.
If you don't drink, skip the wine and just use all chicken broth. The dish will be less complex but still good.
Capers are essential here — don't sub them out. They provide the brininess that defines piccata.
This pairs well over angel hair pasta (toss pasta in the extra sauce) or just eaten with a simple green salad.

name: Pasta al Pomodoro
description: The simplest Italian pasta, and the one where ingredient quality matters most. No meat, no cream — just canned tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, and fresh basil. When the ingredients are good, this is stunning. Use the best canned tomatoes you can find (San Marzano if available), good olive oil, and actual fresh basil — dried basil does not work here. Two paths: a quick 15-minute weeknight version that's light and bright, or a slow-cooked 45-minute version that gets deeper, slightly jammy, and more complex.

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked in olive oil at the start — cook low and slow until fragrant, not burnt

ingredients.name: garlic, thinly sliced or lightly smashed

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic
alternatives.note: add carefully, it can brown faster

ingredients.name: olive oil
ingredients.note: use good olive oil — this is the sauce

ingredients.name: red chili flakes
ingredients.note: optional — adds warmth without much heat

ingredientGroups.label: Sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added once the garlic is fragrant

ingredients.name: canned crushed tomatoes
ingredients.note: San Marzano if possible — noticeably sweeter and less acidic

alternatives.name: whole peeled canned tomatoes
alternatives.note: crush by hand before adding

ingredients.name: sugar
ingredients.note: optional — add if the tomatoes taste sharp or acidic

ingredientGroups.label: Pasta
ingredientGroups.actionNote: boil in well-salted water; reserve pasta water before draining

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added completely off heat at the very end

ingredients.name: fresh basil leaves, torn (not chopped)
ingredients.note: tearing is gentler and preserves more aroma

ingredients.name: good olive oil, drizzled raw
ingredients.note: finishing with unheated olive oil adds flavor that cooked oil loses

steps: Cook pasta in well-salted water.

Thinly slice or lightly smash garlic. In a wide pan over medium heat, warm olive oil. Add garlic and chili flakes. Cook low and slow until garlic is golden and fragrant — ~3 minutes. Watch it; golden is sweet, brown is bitter.
Add crushed tomatoes. Season with salt. Simmer for 10–12 minutes until slightly thickened. Taste — add a pinch of sugar if needed.
Reserve pasta water before draining. Drain pasta and add directly to the sauce. Toss, adding a splash of pasta water to loosen. Cook together 1 minute.
Off heat — tear in fresh basil and drizzle with raw olive oil. Serve immediately.
Follow steps 1–2 of the quick version.

After adding tomatoes, reduce heat to low. Simmer uncovered for 35–40 minutes, stirring occasionally. The sauce will reduce, deepen in color, and become slightly jammy. Add a splash of water if it gets too thick.
Proceed from step 4 of the quick version.

protein.note: from pasta only; intentionally low — treat as a base or side; easy additions are pan-seared shrimp (+25–30g), grilled chicken (+30–40g), or a fried egg (+7g)

tips.text: The tomatoes are 80% of this dish. San Marzano (look for "DOP" on the label) are sweeter, less watery, and less acidic. They are worth it here.
Dried basil cannot replace fresh in this dish — it tastes musty and adds nothing. If you don't have fresh basil, skip it entirely rather than substituting dried.

tips.keyword: Raw olive oil at the end
tips.text: Finishing with a drizzle of good olive oil that hasn't been heated adds a grassy, fruity flavor that cooked olive oil loses. Don't skip this step.
Tearing preserves more of the volatile aromatics and keeps the presentation clean. Chopping bruises it and turns the edges black quickly.

tips.keyword: The slow version is a different dish
tips.text: The 45-minute simmer collapses the tomato structure and concentrates the flavor significantly — it becomes jammy, slightly sweet, and deeply savory. Worth the extra time when you have it.

name: Chicken Tinga
description: Smoky, chipotle-braised shredded chicken. The kind of thing that works in tacos, burrito bowls, over rice, in lettuce wraps — basically anything. The flavor comes from chipotles in adobo, which add a deep, smoky heat. Very easy to batch-cook and use throughout the week.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Chipotle-tomato sauce
ingredientGroups.actionNote: blend or finely chop together and set aside

ingredients.name: chipotle peppers in adobo + 1–2 tbsp of the adobo sauce
ingredients.note: canned — find at Asian or Latin grocery stores; 2 peppers for medium heat, 3 for more

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 2 — Spice mix
ingredientGroups.actionNote: combine dry and set aside

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook whole, then shred with two forks after cooling slightly

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: sautéed in the pan before adding sauce

ingredientGroups.label: Liquid
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added with the shredded chicken to simmer and meld

alternatives.name: water + small piece of bouillon cube dissolved

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: pick your format

alternatives.name: rice + beans
alternatives.note: for a bowl

alternatives.name: lettuce leaves
alternatives.note: for wraps

ingredients.name: toppings — diced onion, cilantro, lime, sliced avocado, jalapeño

instructions.step: Season chicken with salt and pepper. In a pan, cook chicken over medium heat in a little oil until cooked through (~12–15 min for whole breasts). Set aside to cool, then shred with two forks.
While chicken cooks, blend (or finely chop) the chipotle peppers and adobo sauce with the canned tomatoes until smooth-ish.
Slice onion thin and mince garlic. In the same pan, sauté onion over medium heat ~4–5 min until softened. Add garlic, cumin, and oregano. Cook 1 min.
Pour in the tomato-chipotle sauce. Stir and simmer ~5 min.
Add shredded chicken and broth. Stir to combine and simmer ~8–10 min until the sauce thickens and the flavors meld.
Taste and adjust salt. Serve in your preferred format.

protein.note: per serving (2 of 3 servings from this batch)

tips.text: Are smoky and moderately spicy. Use 2 peppers for medium heat, 3 for more. Start conservative — you can always add.
Makes 2–3 servings, keeps in the fridge 4–5 days, and gets better overnight as the flavors settle.
Is easiest while it's still warm (not hot). Two forks work fine; a stand mixer with a paddle attachment shreds it in 30 seconds.

name: Chipotle Ground Beef Bowl
description: Ground beef, drained and rinsed to cut the grease, then hit with chipotle, cumin, garlic, and tomato. Served over rice with whatever toppings you have. A meal-prep-friendly, weeknight go-to — simple, filling, and extremely adjustable.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Spice mix
ingredientGroups.actionNote: combine dry and set aside

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cook first, drain fat, optionally rinse, then continue

alternatives.name: ground turkey or ground chicken
alternatives.note: leaner; same seasoning profile works

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added to the pan after draining the beef

ingredients.name: chipotle peppers in adobo, minced + 1 tbsp adobo sauce

ingredientGroups.label: Tomato & liquid
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added after aromatics are softened

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve underneath

ingredientGroups.label: Toppings
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add at serving — optional but recommended

instructions.step: Cook ground beef in a pan over medium-high heat, breaking it up. Cook until no longer pink.
Drain fat thoroughly. Optional — do a water rinse by adding a cup of hot water to the pan, stir, then drain through a fine strainer. Return beef to pan.
Dice onion and mince garlic. Add onion and cook ~3 min until softened. Add garlic and cook 1 more min.
Mince chipotle peppers. Add chipotle, adobo sauce, cumin, paprika, oregano, salt, and pepper. Stir to coat.
Add tomato paste and a splash of water or broth. Stir and cook ~3–4 min until everything is combined and slightly thickened.
Serve over rice with your toppings.

protein.note: per serving; add a fried or soft-boiled egg for an extra ~6g

tips.text: Is optional — it removes more fat but doesn't meaningfully affect protein content.

tips.keyword: Chipotle in adobo can vary in heat
tips.text: Taste the sauce as you add it and adjust the number of peppers accordingly.
Swap beef for ground turkey (leaner) or ground chicken — all work with the same seasoning profile.

tips.keyword: Scale up easily for meal prep
tips.text: Double the recipe and it keeps for 4 days in the fridge.

name: Salsa Rice Burrito Bowl
description: Salsa-cooked rice toasted with aromatics and spices, built into a bowl with cheddar, Greek yogurt, and optional beans and corn. Minimal effort, maximum flavour.

ingredients.name: rice
ingredients.note: short-grain or long-grain parboiled both work

ingredients.name: canned black beans or pinto beans

instructions.step: Dice onion and mince garlic. Heat oil in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Add onion and cook until softened, about 4–5 minutes. Add garlic and cook 1 more minute until fragrant.
Add the uncooked rice and stir with the aromatics for 2 minutes to toast it — adds a subtle nuttiness.
Pour in the salsa, water, and all seasonings. Stir to combine and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to low, cover, and cook until rice is done — about 18 minutes for long-grain, 20–22 for short-grain. Don't lift the lid. Rest covered for 5 minutes off the heat.
If using beans and/or corn, stir them in during the last 5 minutes of cooking or heat separately and add as toppings.
Scoop rice into bowls. Top with shredded cheddar (melts slightly from the heat), a generous dollop of Greek yogurt, and a squeeze of lime.

tips.text: Keep the salsa-to-water ratio consistent when scaling — roughly ¾ cup salsa to 1¼ cups water per cup of rice.
Not essential. Skip it, or substitute ½ tsp thyme, ½ tsp Italian seasoning, or a pinch of marjoram.
Short-grain gives a stickier, more cohesive base. Long-grain parboiled stays fluffier and more separated — both work well.
Full-fat Greek yogurt works better here than expected — tangier and thicker than sour cream. Low-fat is fine too.

name: Chicken Tikka (Marinated & Grilled Chicken)
description: Tender chunks of chicken marinated in yogurt and spices, then charred under a broiler or on a grill. This is the preparation that goes into tikka masala (if you want to add a sauce) but it's also excellent completely on its own — just the marinated, charred chicken with some sliced onion, a squeeze of lime, and cilantro. High protein, not heavy, genuinely delicious. The yogurt marinade tenderizes the chicken and keeps it moist even on high heat.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Marinade
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together, coat chicken, marinate 2h–overnight

ingredients.name: plain yogurt (full-fat works best for grilling)

alternatives.name: jarred minced ginger
alternatives.note: or grate directly from a frozen knob

alternatives.name: bottled lemon juice
alternatives.note: fine for a marinade

ingredients.name: Kashmiri chili powder or mild paprika

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add to marinade and toss well

ingredients.name: chicken breast, cut into large chunks

alternatives.name: boneless chicken thighs
alternatives.note: more flavor and slightly more forgiving on high heat

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: serve alongside

ingredients.name: sliced raw onion, soaked in cold water 5 min
mint yogurt dip (yogurt + mint + a pinch of salt)

instructions.step: Cut chicken into large chunks. Mince or grate garlic and grate ginger. Mix all marinade ingredients. Add chicken and coat thoroughly. Marinate in the fridge at least 2 hours; overnight is significantly better.
When ready to cook, remove chicken from fridge 15 min early.

steps: Set oven to broil. Place chicken on a foil-lined baking tray.
Broil on high, ~10–12 min total, flipping once halfway. You want some char on the edges — that's correct, not burnt.
Heat pan on very high heat. Add chicken in a single layer with minimal oil.
Don't move it for ~3–4 min to get a sear. Flip and cook other side ~3–4 min.
Finish in oven at 200°C if chunks are thick.

instructions.step: Rest 5 min. Serve with sliced onion, lime, cilantro, and mint yogurt dip.

protein.note: per serving with chicken breast; adding a yogurt dip adds another 10–15g

tips.keyword: The char is intentional and essential
tips.text: It's part of the flavor. Don't be afraid of the dark spots.
It gives the characteristic red-orange color without excessive heat. Substitute with regular paprika or mild chili powder.
Marinate a batch, freeze in the marinade, then thaw and cook when needed.

tips.keyword: To turn this into tikka masala
tips.text: Sauté onion, garlic, ginger, blend with canned tomato, add cream or yogurt and garam masala, then add the cooked tikka. That's the whole dish.

name: Keema (Indian Spiced Ground Meat)
description: Keema is a dry-ish spiced ground meat dish — one of the most satisfying things you can make with ground beef or lamb. The whole spice base (cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic) does the work. This is exactly the right dish for the drain-and-rinse technique: remove the fat, keep the meat, let the spices carry everything. Serve with rice, roti, naan, or just eat it with a spoon.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Bloom spices
ingredientGroups.actionNote: combine dry; added to pan with aromatics to bloom in oil

ingredients.name: smoked paprika or Kashmiri chili powder

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: cooked first, fat drained, set aside; returned to pan after spice base is built

ingredients.name: ground beef or lamb
ingredients.note: lamb is richer; beef works great

ingredientGroups.label: Aromatics
ingredientGroups.actionNote: sautéed together in oil until onion is golden

alternatives.name: jarred minced ginger
alternatives.note: or grate directly from a frozen knob

ingredientGroups.label: Tomato
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added after spices bloom; cook until darkened

ingredientGroups.label: Add-ins
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added once meat is returned to pan

ingredients.name: frozen peas
ingredients.note: optional but traditional; add straight from frozen

ingredientGroups.label: Finish
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added completely off heat

ingredients.name: garam masala
ingredients.note: always added at the end — cooking it too long kills the fragrance

alternatives.name: bottled lemon juice
alternatives.note: fine here since it's stirred in at the end of a hot dish

ingredients.name: plain yogurt on top
ingredients.note: optional — balances the heat and adds protein

instructions.step: Cook ground meat in a pan over medium-high heat. Break it up as it cooks. Once done, drain fat and rinse if preferred. Set aside.

Finely dice onion. Mince garlic, grate or mince ginger, and mince green chili if using. In the same pan, sauté onion in a little oil over medium heat until golden, ~5–7 min. Don't rush this — browned onion is flavor.
Add garlic, ginger, and green chili. Cook ~2 min.
Add cumin, coriander, turmeric, and paprika. Stir and cook ~1 min — the spices bloom in the oil. This is an important step.
Add tomato paste or diced tomato. Cook ~3 min until it darkens slightly.
Return ground meat to the pan. Stir to coat everything in the spice base.
Add frozen peas if using. Add a splash of water if too dry. Simmer ~5–8 min for flavors to meld.
Off heat, add garam masala, lemon juice, and cilantro. Taste and adjust salt.

protein.note: per serving; a side of plain yogurt adds another ~10–15g and balances the heat

tips.text: Is the difference between a flat keema and a good one. Medium heat, patience, ~6–7 min until golden.

tips.keyword: Garam masala goes in at the end
tips.text: Cooking it too long kills the fragrance. Add it off heat and stir through.

tips.keyword: Keema is excellent the next day
tips.text: Reheat with a splash of water. Great for meal prep.
Like a spiced sloppy joe — an underrated quick lunch option.

name: Jollof Rice
description: The dish. A one-pot rice cooked directly in a seasoned tomato-pepper base — it absorbs everything as it steams. The hallmark of good jollof is the smoky bottom crust (the "party jollof" layer) that forms when the liquid runs out and the rice toasts slightly against the pot. Don't be alarmed when you smell it — that's the goal. Serve with grilled or fried chicken, fried plantains, coleslaw, or whatever protein you like.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Blender base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: blend until smooth and set aside

ingredients.name: jarred roasted red peppers, drained (~equivalent of 2 red bell peppers)

alternatives.name: frozen bell pepper strips
alternatives.note: both work fine once blended

alternatives.name: scotch bonnet / habanero peppers
alternatives.note: very hot — add cautiously

ingredients.name: onion, roughly chopped
ingredients.note: blender only — NOT the same as the sliced onion below

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 2 — Spice mix
ingredientGroups.actionNote: combine dry and set aside

ingredientGroups.label: Cup — Liquid
ingredientGroups.actionNote: prepare before you start cooking; pick one, not both

ingredients.name: chicken stock (carton)
ingredients.note: next time consider starting with 350ml — 400ml was too much on first attempt

alternatives.name: water + 1 Maggi/Knorr/Aurora bouillon cube dissolved
alternatives.note: consider half a cube if using no-salt-added tomatoes — a full cube made it slightly too salty on first attempt

ingredientGroups.label: Pot ingredients
ingredientGroups.actionNote: added during cooking in this order

ingredients.name: onion, thinly sliced
ingredients.note: pot only — NOT the blender onion

ingredientGroups.label: Rice
ingredientGroups.actionNote: rinse under cold water until the water runs clear; drain just before adding

ingredients.name: long-grain parboiled rice
ingredients.note: must say "parboiled" or "converted" on the bag — No Name, Uncle Ben's Original, or PC parboiled. Do NOT use jasmine, basmati, or regular long grain.

instructions.step: Blend the base: blend tomatoes, bell peppers, scotch bonnet, and onion until smooth. Set aside.

Slice the pot onion thinly (not the blender onion). Fry the tomato base: heat oil in a wide, heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high heat. Add onion and fry ~3 min. Add tomato paste and stir-fry ~2 min until it darkens slightly.

Pour in the blended tomato mixture. It will splatter — stir immediately and reduce heat to medium. Let this fry/simmer uncovered, stirring occasionally, for 20–25 min until it deepens in color, the raw tomato smell is gone, and the oil starts to float on top. This step is not optional — undercooked tomato base = sour jollof.
Season the base: add curry powder, thyme, salt, white pepper, and bay leaves. Stir and cook 2 more min.

Add stock and rice: pour in chicken stock. Bring to a boil. Taste the liquid — it should be well-seasoned. Add the washed and drained rice. Stir once to distribute. If the pot looks too wet, simmer uncovered for a minute or two before putting the lid on.
Steam the rice: reduce heat to low. Cover the pot tightly (use foil under the lid for a better seal if your lid isn't tight). Cook ~30 min without lifting the lid.

Check and finish: after 30 min, the rice should be cooked and liquid mostly absorbed. If still soupy, re-cover and cook another 5–10 min. For the smoky bottom crust, turn heat to medium-low for ~5 min. You'll hear a light sizzle and smell a subtle smokiness. Remove from heat.
Stir from the bottom to incorporate the crust throughout. Remove bay leaves. Rest 5 min, then serve.

protein.note: jollof rice itself is mostly carbs — serve with protein on the side (spiced chicken, chicken tikka, etc.)

tips.text: Jasmine or regular white rice will turn to mush. Look for "converted" or "parboiled" on the bag. Uncle Ben's Original is the most common; PC and No Name both make parboiled versions.

If you can find a tube, great — lasts months in the fridge. If buying a can (more common), get the smallest size (156ml) and freeze the rest in 1 tbsp portions in an ice cube tray. Use straight from frozen.
If you skipped them and used cayenne instead, start with ¼ tsp and taste before adding more — easier to add heat than take it away.

tips.keyword: The tomato-frying step is the whole game
tips.text: Raw, under-fried tomato base makes the rice taste sour and thin. Fry until the oil separates from the tomato — that's your cue it's done.
Steam is everything. Every time you open the pot you lose steam and extend cooking time.
Is called "party jollof" in Nigerian cooking — it's the most prized part. If you get it, you did it right. Scrape it up and mix it in.

notes: Too much liquid — used 400ml stock plus topped up with extra water during cooking due to lid not being fully sealed. Rice came out clumpy. Next time start with 350ml and don't top up.
Lid seal — lid wasn't airtight so steam escaped throughout, which triggered the urge to add water. Try to seal tighter, or use foil under the lid as the recipe suggests.
Tomato sauce pooled on top after the 30 min steam — normal when lid isn't fully sealed, just stir it in.
Clumpy texture — caused by too much liquid and not rinsing the rice enough. Next time rinse until the water runs clear.
Slightly too salty — the bouillon cube is the main salt source and it's quite salty on its own. Consider using half a cube next time, especially if using no-salt-added tomatoes.
If the pot looks too wet when you add the rice — simmer uncovered for a minute or two first before putting the lid on.

name: Kelewele
description: Ghanaian spiced fried plantains — hot, fragrant, and addictive. Ripe plantains tossed in ginger, chili, and anise before frying until caramelized with slightly charred edges. Better than plain maduros when you want heat and complexity alongside the sweetness. Street food, but quick enough to make any night.

ingredientGroups.label: Spice Mix
ingredientGroups.actionNote: combine in a small bowl before you start on the plantains

ingredients.name: anise seeds, lightly crushed
ingredients.note: the signature kelewele flavor — adds a warm, fennel-like fragrance; crush lightly in your palm to release oils

alternatives.name: ground cloves
alternatives.note: less aromatic but works if you don't have anise

ingredientGroups.label: Plantains
ingredientGroups.actionNote: peel, slice, and toss in spice mix just before frying

ingredients.name: ripe plantains
ingredients.note: yellow with heavy black spotting — ripe enough to be sweet but still firm; fully black is too soft and will burn

instructions.step: Peel plantains, slice into 1-inch diagonal pieces.
Toss with the spice mix until evenly coated.
Heat oil in a pan over medium-high. Add plantains in a single layer — don't crowd; fry in batches if needed.
Fry 2–3 min per side until dark golden with caramelized, slightly charred edges. The sugars will smoke a bit — that's fine.
Drain briefly on paper towel and serve immediately.

protein.note: this is a carb/snack side — ~30–35g carbs per serving

tips.text: Yellow with heavy black spotting is the target. Ripe enough to be sweet and soft-inside, but still firm enough to hold shape in the pan. Fully black plantains are too mushy and will burn before caramelizing.
Fry in batches if your pan is small. Crowding drops the oil temperature and steams the plantains instead of frying them — you lose the char.

Are what makes kelewele distinctive. Find them at South Asian or African grocery stores (often labelled "anise" or "star anise seeds" — use the small seeds, not whole star anise). Lightly crush in your palm to release the oils before adding.
Tongs or kitchen tweezers work better than a spatula here — you're turning smaller irregular chunks and want to grab individual pieces rather than scooping.
Kelewele is best hot. It softens and loses the texture quickly as it cools.

name: Spiced Chicken (Jollof Pairing)
description: Simple seasoned chicken breast built to go with jollof rice. The spice blend mirrors the flavors already in the jollof — curry powder, thyme, paprika — so everything on the plate tastes like it belongs together. Crispy outside, juicy inside from the air fryer.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Spice rub
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix into a paste, rub all over chicken

alternatives.name: jarred minced garlic, rubbed onto the chicken
skip entirely
alternatives.note: for no heat

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: use either salt OR the bouillon cube — not both (too salty together, per first attempt)

ingredients.name: bouillon cube (Maggi/Knorr/Aurora), crumbled dry
ingredients.note: crumble directly into the spice mix — do NOT dissolve in water first; optional but recommended

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: pat dry before applying rub

steps: Pat chicken dry with a paper towel — this helps the crust form.
Mix all spices and oil together into a paste. Rub all over the chicken, coating evenly.
Air fry at 200°C (400°F) for 18–22 min, flipping once halfway. Exact time depends on thickness — check that the thickest part is cooked through (no pink). A thicker breast may need the full 22 min.
Rest 3–5 min before slicing or serving. Don't skip this — it keeps the juices in.
Heat a pan over medium-high with a little oil.
Sear chicken 5–6 min per side until cooked through and deeply browned.
Cover with a lid for the last 2 min if the breast is thick.
Rest 3–5 min before serving.

protein.note: per serving (one large chicken breast)

tips.keyword: The bouillon cube in the rub sounds unusual
tips.text: But it's a common West African trick — it adds a savory depth that plain salt doesn't. Just crumble it finely and mix it in with the spices. Do not dissolve it in water first.
Slice horizontally almost all the way through and open flat for more even cooking and better crust surface area.
Slice well cold and work in wraps or on rice the next day.

notes: Too salty — using both the bouillon cube and added salt was too much. Next time use either the cube or the salt, not both. The cube alone is enough.
Watery paste — bouillon cube was accidentally dissolved in water before mixing. The cube should be crumbled dry directly into the spice mix — no water needed.
Chicken itself tasted good and had a solid crust despite the paste being watery going in. The air fryer sorted it out.

name: Jerk Chicken Bowl
description: Bold, fiery, smoky jerk chicken — fragrant with allspice, scotch bonnet, and thyme — served over rice or alongside tostones or maduros. This is the real flavor profile: not just spicy, but deeply aromatic with warm spices and a slight sweetness from brown sugar. The char is intentional.

ingredientGroups.label: Jerk Marinade
ingredientGroups.actionNote: blend or finely mince everything into a paste; marinate chicken at least 30 min (overnight is dramatically better)

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet or habanero pepper
ingredients.note: 1 for medium heat, 2 for hot; remove seeds for milder

alternatives.name: serrano peppers
alternatives.note: less fruity than scotch bonnet but closer than jalapeño

alternatives.name: ground ginger
alternatives.note: less bright but works fine

alternatives.name: frozen ginger, grated from frozen
alternatives.note: freeze a knob whole and grate straight from the freezer

ingredients.name: ground allspice
ingredients.note: the defining flavor of jerk — don't skip it

alternatives.name: bottled lime juice
alternatives.note: shelf-stable

ingredientGroups.label: Protein
ingredientGroups.actionNote: coat in marinade and refrigerate; bring to room temp 15 min before cooking

ingredients.name: chicken thighs
ingredients.note: skin-on adds flavor and char; boneless/skinless is easier to slice

alternatives.name: chicken breasts
alternatives.note: leaner but dries out faster — reduce cook time by ~3 min per side

ingredientGroups.label: Serving
ingredientGroups.actionNote: pick your carb and any extras; cook separately

ingredients.name: white rice
ingredients.note: cooked separately; neutral base that soaks up the jus

ingredients.name: tostones
ingredients.note: see Tostones recipe — crispy, savory, pairs well with the savory heat

alternatives.name: maduros
alternatives.note: see Maduros recipe — sweeter contrast if you want it

ingredients.name: quick cabbage slaw — shredded cabbage, lime juice, pinch of salt
canned black beans, warmed
ingredients.note: optional; adds fiber and rounds it out as a bowl

steps: Blend all marinade ingredients into a rough paste. A small blender or food processor is easiest; finely mincing by hand works but is messier.
Score the chicken thighs with a few shallow cuts so the marinade penetrates. Coat thoroughly.
Marinate in the fridge at least 30 min. Overnight is dramatically better — the flavor goes from surface-level to deep.
Pull chicken from the fridge 15 min before cooking to take the chill off.
Heat a heavy pan (cast iron is ideal) over medium-high with a thin film of oil. When smoking, add chicken in a single layer.
Cook 5–7 min per side for boneless thighs until deeply charred and cooked through (74°C / 165°F internal). The outside should look almost burnt — that caramelized crust is correct.
Rest 5 min before slicing.
Slice chicken and serve over rice, or alongside tostones or maduros.
If making slaw, toss cabbage with lime juice and salt right before serving — it wilts fast.

protein.note: per serving with 2 boneless chicken thighs (~160–180g each)

tips.text: Is the non-negotiable flavor of jerk. It smells like cinnamon, cloves, and pepper combined — that's the point. Don't skip it or substitute with a pre-made "jerk seasoning blend" as your only flavor source.
Is intentional. Jerk chicken should look almost burnt from the sugar and marinade caramelizing. A pale pan-sear means the heat was too low.
Has a fruity heat that habanero approximates but serrano or jalapeño don't. Worth seeking out at a Caribbean, African, or South Asian grocery if you want the authentic flavor profile.
Makes a genuine difference here more than in most recipes. The allspice and thyme need time to work into the meat. If you're doing meal prep, marinate the night before.

name: Maduros
description: Caramelized sweet fried plantains — one of the simplest, most satisfying things you can make. The key is ripe plantains (mostly-black skin) so the sugars are fully developed. Pairs with jerk chicken, rice and beans, or anything that benefits from a sweet contrast. A legitimate carb source at ~35g per serving.

ingredientGroups.label: Plantains
ingredientGroups.actionNote: peel and slice just before cooking — they oxidize quickly once cut

ingredients.name: ripe plantains
ingredients.note: skin should be mostly yellow-black to fully black; green or yellow won't caramelize

ingredients.name: neutral oil
ingredients.note: enough to coat the pan; not deep fry

instructions.step: Peel plantains — cut off both ends, score the skin lengthwise, and peel off. Slice on the diagonal into ¾–1 inch pieces.
Heat oil in a non-stick or cast iron pan over medium heat. When shimmering, add plantain slices in a single layer without crowding.
Cook 2–3 min per side without moving them, until deep golden brown and caramelized. They should release easily when ready — if they stick, they need more time.
Transfer to paper towel briefly. Finish with flaky salt and a squeeze of lime if desired.

protein.note: this is a carb side — ~30–35g carbs per serving, roughly equivalent to a medium potato

tips.text: Is everything here. A yellow plantain fries okay but won't caramelize properly. A mostly-black plantain will be sweet, soft, and deeply flavored. Buy yellow and wait 3–5 days at room temp.
Not high. High heat burns the outside before the inside softens. Medium gives the sugars time to caramelize without scorching.
Let them sit undisturbed for the full 2–3 minutes. Touching them too early tears the developing crust.
Use a thin spatula (fish spatula is ideal). Tongs or tweezers will dent or break the soft flesh when you grip the edge — you need to slide under the whole slice and flip cleanly.

¾–1 inch is the target. Thinner and the inside cooks dry before the outside caramelizes — you lose the jammy center. Thicker and the outside scorches before the center softens. The diagonal cut helps too — it increases the flat caramelizing surface area relative to the thickness, so you get more browning per bite.

name: Tostones
description: Twice-fried green plantains — crispy, salty, savory, and satisfying. Smashed flat between two fries, they come out dense and crunchy like a thick plantain chip. A near-universal Latin American and Caribbean side, and the best use of an unripe plantain. Optional garlic mojo takes them up another level.

ingredientGroups.label: Plantains
ingredientGroups.actionNote: peel by cutting off both ends, scoring the skin lengthwise 3–4 times, and working your fingers under it — the skin is stiff and will resist

ingredients.name: green plantains
ingredients.note: fully green or very slightly yellow; yellow plantains are too soft and won't crisp

ingredients.name: neutral oil
ingredients.note: enough for a shallow fry, about ½ cm depth in the pan

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: season immediately after the second fry while still hot and oily — it won't stick once cool

ingredientGroups.label: Mojo (optional)
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together just before serving; drizzle or dip

alternatives.name: bottled lime juice
alternatives.note: shelf-stable

steps: Peel the green plantains and slice into rounds about 1 inch thick.
Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Add plantain slices and fry 2–3 min per side until pale golden — you're softening them, not crisping. They should feel slightly yielding when pressed.
Remove and drain on paper towel.
While still hot, place each slice between two pieces of parchment or plastic wrap and smash flat to about ½ cm thick. Use the bottom of a heavy mug, a small cutting board, or a tostonera.
You want a rough, irregular disc with some texture — not a paper-thin chip.
Increase heat to medium-high. Return smashed tostones to the oil and fry 2 min per side until deeply golden and crispy.
Drain on paper towel and season with salt immediately.
Drizzle with mojo or serve as-is.

protein.note: this is a starchy carb side — ~35–40g carbs per serving

tips.text: Yellow plantains will fall apart when smashed and turn mushy instead of crispy. You need the starch of a fully green or very slightly yellow plantain to hold the structure through the second fry.
Salt right after the second fry while still hot. It won't adhere once the surface cools and dries.

The skin is stiff and grips tightly. Cut off both ends, score lengthwise 3–4 times, then work your fingers or a spoon under the skin. A brief soak in warm salted water (5 min) can loosen stubborn skins.

Spatula for both fries. In the first fry the rounds are soft, so get under the whole slice rather than gripping the edge. After smashing they're firm enough that tongs work too, but a spatula is cleaner since you can slide under the full surface.

1 inch before smashing is intentional. You're compressing roughly 2:1, so the smashed disc ends up with enough body to crisp through without being too dense. Too thin before smashing gives you a paper-thin disc that burns in the second fry before the center heats. Too thick and it smashes unevenly and stays soft in the middle when the outside is done.
Firm but not shattering — you want a rough disc, not crumbs. Some cracks are fine and add crunch surface area.

The ½ cm shallow pool isn't arbitrary — it lets oil contact the sides of the disc, not just the flat bottom. A thin film like you'd use for a fried egg only fries the contact surface; the edges steam instead of crisping. For maduros (where you want soft and jammy) a thin film is fine. For tostones, where the goal is full crunch including the edges, the shallow pool matters especially on the second fry.

name: Breakfast Smoothie (Base + Variations)
description: High-protein breakfast smoothie built around naked (unflavored) protein powder — so the fruit carries all the flavor. The base is the same every time; what changes is the fruit. Since there's no sweetness from the powder, riper and sweeter fruit matters more here than it would with vanilla powder. Banana + avocado is the richest, creamiest version. Berry mixes need a small amount of honey to compensate for the tartness.

ingredientGroups.label: Fruit base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: microwave 60–90 seconds to soften before blending

ingredients.name: frozen fruit
ingredients.note: any No Frills frozen mix works — berry, tropical, mixed; microwave briefly before blending

alternatives.name: banana + avocado (fresh, frozen)
alternatives.note: the richest, creamiest version; no sweetener needed since banana carries it

ingredientGroups.label: Liquid and protein base
ingredientGroups.actionNote: add to blender first — liquid goes in before everything else

alternatives.name: oat milk or soy milk
alternatives.note: if keeping it dairy-light

ingredientGroups.label: Optional add-ins
ingredientGroups.actionNote: blend in with everything else

ingredients.name: honey
ingredients.note: only if the fruit isn't sweet enough — see sweetening notes in tips

alternatives.name: Medjool date, pitted
alternatives.note: blends smooth, very sweet, adds fiber

ingredients.name: handful of spinach
ingredients.note: adds basically no flavor, bumps nutrients; frozen spinach works

ingredients.name: nut butter
ingredients.note: adds healthy fat and makes it more filling; pairs well with banana combos

instructions.step: Microwave frozen fruit 60–90 seconds. Let it sit a moment — you want softened, not hot.
Add liquid to the Ninja cup first (helps blending), then yogurt, protein powder, and fruit.
Blend until smooth. If too thick, add a splash more milk. If too thin, add more yogurt.
Drink immediately.

protein.note: Greek yogurt (~15g) + protein powder (~25g) + milk (~7g); one of the highest-protein breakfasts you can make with minimal effort

tips.text: Sweet mixes (mango, pineapple, peach-heavy) are usually fine without sweetener. Tart mixes (berry-heavy) likely need ½–1 tsp honey — naked powder has no sweetness to fall back on so tart fruit can taste flat or slightly chalky. If the mix includes banana, no sweetener needed at all.
In the Ninja — it prevents the powder from clumping at the bottom and helps everything blend smoothly.

Order fresh bananas and avocados on PC Express, freeze immediately when they arrive. Peel and slice bananas; scoop and portion avocados when just ripe. Freeze flat. Eliminates ripeness timing stress and makes this combo as convenient as any other.
If naked powder tends to clump, mix it with the milk in the cup first with a spoon before adding everything else.
Microwave briefly (not fully thawing) gives you a cold, thick smoothie without destroying the blender motor on rock-solid frozen chunks.

name: Banana Bread
description: A moist, not-too-sweet banana bread that comes together in one bowl. Uses Greek yogurt for extra moisture and a small protein bump — and optionally swaps some flour for unflavored whey if you want to push the protein higher. Best with very ripe bananas (black-spotted or fully black skin) — the riper they are, the sweeter and more flavorful the bread. Bananas from the freezer work perfectly here.

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 1 — Wet ingredients
ingredientGroups.actionNote: mix together first

ingredients.name: very ripe bananas, mashed (~300g peeled)
ingredients.note: frozen and thawed works great — drain any liquid before mashing

alternatives.name: melted butter
alternatives.note: slightly richer, slightly denser

ingredients.name: plain Greek yogurt
ingredients.note: adds moisture and a little protein

ingredients.name: sugar
ingredients.note: 80g is lightly sweet; 100g is standard sweet

alternatives.name: honey or maple syrup
alternatives.note: reduce oil by 1 tbsp if using

ingredientGroups.label: Bowl 2 — Dry ingredients
ingredientGroups.actionNote: whisk together, then fold into Bowl 1

alternatives.name: unflavored whey protein powder
alternatives.note: boosts protein per slice to ~10–12g; texture is slightly denser but still good. Do not exceed 30g whey or it gets rubbery.

ingredientGroups.label: Add-ins
ingredientGroups.actionNote: fold in last — optional

instructions.step: Preheat oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease a 9×5 inch (23×13 cm) loaf pan lightly with oil or butter. Line with a strip of parchment paper if you have it — makes removal easy.
Mash bananas in a large bowl until mostly smooth (a few small lumps are fine). Add eggs, oil, Greek yogurt, sugar, and vanilla. Stir until combined.

Add dry ingredients: sprinkle the flour (or flour + whey), baking soda, salt, and cinnamon directly into the wet bowl. Fold gently with a spatula until just combined — stop as soon as you can't see dry flour. Overmixing = tough bread.
Fold in add-ins if using.
Pour into loaf pan. Smooth the top. Optional — sprinkle a few banana slices or a pinch of sugar on top for a nicer crust.

Bake 55–65 min until a toothpick or knife inserted into the center comes out clean (a few moist crumbs are fine; wet batter means it needs more time). If the top is browning too fast around 40 min, loosely tent with foil.
Cool in pan 10 min, then turn out onto a rack. Slice only once fully cool — warm banana bread is crumbly and falls apart.

protein.note: per slice (standard); ~10–12g per slice with the whey substitution

tips.text: If your bananas aren't black-spotted yet, speed-ripen them — bake unpeeled at 150°C (300°F) for 15–20 min until the skin blackens and they're soft. Cool before using.
Thaw in the fridge overnight or on the counter for an hour. Drain any liquid before mashing.
Once the flour goes in, ten to fifteen folds is enough. Lumpy batter is fine.
Ovens vary — start checking at 50 min. If the toothpick comes out with wet batter at 65 min, cover with foil and go another 10 min.
Wrap tightly and leave at room temperature up to 3 days, or freeze individual slices. Reheat from frozen at 150°C (300°F) for ~10 min, or just toast it.
The bread will be slightly denser and less springy than the all-flour version. Still good — just don't expect it to be identical. Add a handful of walnuts if using whey; they improve the texture.
